evals.name: eval-mode-selection
evals.prompt: Hi, I'm a freelance designer and I recently had an idea to build a SaaS tool that helps other freelancers manage their clients and projects. I'm not sure how thorough I need to make this plan — I have limited time but I want to think through the direction clearly before I start. Can you help me plan this?
evals.expected_output: Presents five execution mode options (Quick/Full/Revision/Custom/Build), each with a brief description, asks the user which one they want to choose, and does not start executing any analysis steps.
expectations: Has not started executing any product framework analysis (no JTBD, Persona, PR-FAQ, etc. output produced)
Responds in English

Response presents modes as a clear selection menu (e.g., numbered options or table); does not jump straight into giving directional advice or starting analysis (no JTBD outline, no framework recommendations)
This turn only asks about mode selection, does not simultaneously ask about product type or completeness level (three-step progressive rule)

evals.name: eval-quick-mode-jtbd
evals.prompt: I want to build a B2C knowledge-capture app that lets users quickly jot down key takeaways and ideas from their daily reading, and easily review them later. Please use Quick Mode and execute S1 directly.
evals.expected_output: Executes S1 JTBD analysis in Quick Mode, producing JTBD statements covering Functional, Emotional, and Social types, then pauses after S1 completion to wait for user confirmation before proceeding to S2.
expectations: Displays a progress indicator at the beginning of the response, formatted with 'Quick Mode' and step progress (S1/S3)
JTBD statements follow a structured format such as '[Customer description] + [Situation] + [Job]'
Includes at least three JTBD types: Functional, Emotional, and Social
JTBD analysis includes concrete usage scenario descriptions (not abstract feature lists)
After completing S1, asks the user for confirmation or feedback; does not automatically proceed to S2
The post-S1 question is a meaningful question about JTBD decisions (not a generic 'Any thoughts?' but rather a confirmation about a specific Job or scenario)
Response contains no code creation, file creation, or development commands (Hard Gate compliance: development operations are prohibited during the planning flow)

evals.name: eval-jtbd-depth
evals.prompt: I'm building a B2B HR leave management system that primarily serves two user types: HR Specialists (responsible for maintaining leave policies and approval workflows) and Mid-level Managers (responsible for approving their direct reports' leave requests). Please perform an in-depth JTBD analysis for these two Personas, including the Five Whys deep-dive and a quality self-review checklist.
evals.expected_output: Performs a complete JTBD analysis for both the HR Specialist and the Mid-level Manager, including three JTBD types, Five Whys deep-dive, and a quality self-review checklist with pass/fail markers, and indicates which Persona should be prioritized and why.
expectations: Performs independent JTBD analysis for each of the two Personas: HR Specialist and Mid-level Manager
Includes a complete Five Whys deep-dive with at least 3-5 progressively deeper 'why' questions
Quality self-review checklist marks each item as pass or fail (using checkmark or cross markers)
Clearly states which Persona to prioritize and provides specific reasoning

The final question in the Five Whys (Q5) explicitly uses psychological or emotional vocabulary (e.g., fear, anxiety, shame, worry, dread, threat to professional identity) to describe the emotional root cause — not merely an implied-emotional functional statement (e.g., 'HR credibility is affected' is NOT sufficient; 'HR fears being held accountable during audits — that is a real fear' IS sufficient)
B2B analysis includes organization-level Jobs (e.g., compliance auditing, cross-department approval workflows, cost control — needs beyond individual tasks)
Quality self-review checklist contains at least 1 explicit cross/fail marker (warning markers do not count), and that failed item includes a specific improvement direction

evals.name: eval-prfaq-output
evals.prompt: I'm building a restaurant prep planning tool called 'PrepFlow'. I've already completed the JTBD analysis. The target users are kitchen managers at small restaurants (10-50 seats), and the core JTBD is: 'When it's Friday afternoon and the kitchen manager needs to prep for the weekend rush, they need a tool that can quickly calculate ingredient quantities and auto-generate a purchase order, so they can avoid running short on ingredients or wasting food due to estimation errors.' Please execute PR-FAQ (S2) directly and produce the full PR-FAQ document with quality self-review.
evals.expected_output: A complete PR-FAQ document including a headline written from the user's perspective, a lead paragraph with a concrete scenario, a solution experience description, a realistic user quote, External FAQ (including pointed challenges), Internal FAQ, and a quality self-review checklist.
expectations: PR-FAQ headline is written from the user benefit or experience perspective, not as a feature description
Lead paragraph includes a concrete usage scenario or numbers, not just a feature list

The first sentence of the solution paragraph starts with user experience or a usage scenario, not with a system-feature verb (must not begin with 'PrepFlow lets you', 'The system will', 'Click to', or similar feature-first phrasing)

External FAQ contains at least 1 pointed challenge comparing to existing tools (e.g., 'Why not just keep using Excel or spreadsheets?'), and the answer must explicitly acknowledge at least one specific advantage of the existing tool (e.g., 'Excel is flexible and kitchen staff are already familiar with it') — vague acknowledgments like 'if it works for you, keep using it' are insufficient
User quote reads like a real kitchen worker speaking, not like an official press release
Quality self-review checklist contains at least 1 explicit cross/fail marker (warning markers do not count), and that failed item includes a specific improvement direction

evals.name: eval-revision-mode
evals.prompt: Our product is TeamFlow, a B2B workflow management tool primarily serving mid-sized companies of 50-200 employees. We currently have 2,800 MAU, but retention has dropped from 85% last quarter to 72%. User interviews indicate that feature complexity is the main pain point. I'd like to do a product revision — can you help?
evals.expected_output: Identifies this as a revision scenario, triggers Revision Mode, displays the Revision Mode progress indicator, leverages the data provided in the prompt (MAU 2,800, retention drop from 85% to 72%), lists what additional data needs to be gathered, and explains the Revision Mode workflow structure.
expectations: Displays a Revision Mode progress indicator at the beginning of the response (including the step sequence)
Clearly identifies this as an existing product revision scenario, not a 0-to-1 new product
Lists additional data or information that needs to be collected
Uses the specific data provided in the prompt (MAU 2,800, retention drop from 85% to 72%) in the response
Identifies 'feature complexity' as the core user pain-point hypothesis and reflects it in the analysis
After S1, provides the user with clear action options (numbered menu or specific CTAs), not an open-ended question (e.g., must not end with just 'Any thoughts?')

evals.name: eval-quality-hardgate
evals.prompt: I want to build a B2C fitness habit tracking app that lets busy professionals log their daily workouts and get personalized training recommendations. Please use Quick Mode and execute S1 JTBD analysis, including the Five Whys deep-dive and quality self-review checklist.
evals.expected_output: A complete Quick Mode S1 JTBD analysis with Five Whys deep-dive and quality self-review checklist. The quality self-review checklist must mark each item as pass or fail (no blank checkboxes allowed), and at least 1 item must be marked as failed with a specific improvement explanation.
expectations: Quality self-review checklist uses explicit pass or fail markers for each item (no blank checkboxes allowed)
Quality self-review checklist contains at least 1 explicit cross/fail marker (warning markers do not count) — proving the self-review mechanism genuinely works and is not a rubber stamp
Failed items include a specific improvement explanation (describing how to strengthen it, not just marking it as failed without text)
The final question in the Five Whys (Q5) uses explicit emotional vocabulary (e.g., fear, anxiety, shame, worry, dread, self-doubt, sense of loss) rather than merely describing functional consequences

evals.name: eval-feature-extension
evals.prompt: Our product is an e-commerce platform built with Next.js + PostgreSQL + Redis. The main modules are auth, catalog, cart, checkout, and payment. We want to add a 'Wishlist' feature that lets users save products they're interested in and easily add them to the cart later. Please help me plan this feature.
evals.expected_output: Identifies this as a feature extension scenario, triggers the feature extension fast path (4 steps), displays the 4-step progress indicator, and in S1 confirms the existing system context (tech stack, affected modules) without asking the user to re-describe architecture information already provided.
expectations: Identifies this as a feature extension scenario (not Full Mode or Revision Mode) and activates the 4-step fast path
Displays the feature extension progress indicator at the beginning of the response (S1/S4, not S1/S7 or S1/S12)
Confirms or references the tech stack already provided by the user (Next.js + PostgreSQL + Redis) and the module list, without asking for re-description
Analyzes which existing modules the Wishlist feature would affect (mentions at least cart and catalog)
Pauses after S1 completion to wait for user confirmation; does not automatically proceed to S2
Does not ask the user to provide JTBD, Persona, North Star, or other global product strategy elements unrelated to feature extension

evals.name: eval-security-awareness
evals.prompt: I've completed the product planning (Full Mode) for a B2C social platform. The platform has user registration and login, user profiles, post publishing, real-time messaging, and push notifications. Now I need to generate the developer handoff package. Please produce the developer handoff package.
evals.expected_output: The developer handoff package includes security-related content: mentions authentication/authorization, input validation, CORS, or other security considerations in the architecture or TASKS.md. Should not produce a purely functional handoff package with no security mentions.
expectations: Developer handoff package includes a security-related section or reminders (mentions at least 2 of: authentication/authorization, input validation, CORS, CSP, Rate Limiting)
TASKS.md or the task list includes security-related tasks (not just a vague description buried in the last phase)
Mentions that .gitignore should include sensitive files (.env, credentials, etc.)
Raises security considerations specific to social platforms (at least 1 of: XSS protection, user-uploaded content filtering, real-time message encryption)

evals.name: eval-context-bootstrap
evals.prompt: We have an existing project management tool called TaskPro, but we've never used your product planning tool before. Now we want to add a Gantt chart feature to TaskPro. The tech stack is React + Node.js + MongoDB.
evals.expected_output: Detects this is an existing product's first use (no .product-context.md), performs a Context Bootstrap to collect basic product information (product name, one-line description, type) before entering the feature extension S1, then enters the feature extension flow. Should not skip Bootstrap and jump straight into feature planning.
expectations: Identifies this as a feature extension scenario (adding a new feature to an existing product)
Before feature planning, collects or confirms basic product information (product name TaskPro, product type B2B/B2C, one-line description, etc.)
Confirms or references the tech stack already provided by the user (React + Node.js + MongoDB) without asking for re-description
Enters the 4-step feature extension flow (not the full 7-step or 12-step revision flow)
Mentions that product context will be saved for future use (or references .product-context.md)
